title: Causative & Causative-Passive
learningGoal: The learner will master the Causative form (making/letting someone do something) and the Causative-Passive form (being forced to do something).
instructions: Welcome to the final boss of Japanese verb conjugations! The causative and causative-passive forms are essential for talking about power dynamics, such as bosses making employees work, or parents making children eat vegetables.

lessonContent.bodyText: The causative form is used when one person makes or lets another person do an action. The person who is made/allowed to do the action is usually marked with 'ni'.

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: Ru-Verbs: たべる -> たべさせる (Taberu -> Tabesaseru)
U-Verbs: のむ -> のませる (Nomu -> Nomaseru)
ははは こどもに やさいを たべさせました。
targetLanguageExamples.transliteration: Haha wa kodomo ni yasai wo tabesasemashita.
targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: The mother made the child eat vegetables.

lessonContent.bodyText: As always, 'Suru' (to do) and 'Kuru' (to come) are irregular and must be memorized.

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: くる -> こさせる (Kuru -> Kosaseru)
ぶちょうは わたしに ざんぎょうを させました。
targetLanguageExamples.transliteration: Buchou wa watashi ni zangyou wo sasemashita.
targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: The boss made me do overtime.

lessonContent.bodyText: Japanese has a specific form for when you are forced to do something against your will. This is called the causative-passive. You take the causative form, and turn it into the passive form.

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: Ru-Verbs: たべさせる -> たべさせられる (Tabesaserareru)
わたしは ははに やさいを たべさせられました。
targetLanguageExamples.transliteration: Watashi wa haha ni yasai wo tabesaseraremashita.
targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: I was forced to eat vegetables by my mother. (Note the person forcing you is marked with 'ni').

lessonContent.bodyText: For U-verbs, there is a shortcut. Instead of saying 'Nomaserareru', you can just drop the 'u' and add 'asareru'.

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: のむ -> のまされる (Nomu -> Nomasareru)
targetLanguageExamples.transliteration: To drink -> To be forced to drink

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: いく -> いかされる (Iku -> Ikasareru)
targetLanguageExamples.transliteration: To go -> To be forced to go

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: わたしは くすりを のまされました。
targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: I was forced to drink medicine.

learningObjective: The learner will accurately conjugate verbs into their causative ('make someone do') and causative-passive ('be forced to do') forms, correctly utilizing particles to identify the instigator and the actor.

examples.targetText: ぶちょうは たなかさんを アメリカに いかせました。
examples.transliteration: Buchou wa Tanaka-san wo Amerika ni ikasemashita.
examples.baseTranslation: The boss made Tanaka-san go to America. (Causative)
examples.explanation: Because 'iku' does not take a direct object, the person being made to go is marked with 'wo'.

examples.targetText: たなかさんは ぶちょうに アメリカに いかされました。
examples.transliteration: Tanaka-san wa buchou ni Amerika ni ikasaremashita.
examples.baseTranslation: Tanaka-san was forced to go to America by the boss. (Causative-Passive)
examples.explanation: The subject is Tanaka. The person forcing him is the boss (marked with ni).

importantRules.ruleText: Causative-Passive ALWAYS implies annoyance or being forced against your will.
importantRules.exceptionNotes: Do not use it if you are happy about doing the action. Use 'Te-morau' (I had them let me do it) instead.

importantRules.ruleText: U-verbs ending in 'su' (like Hanasu) cannot use the short causative-passive.
importantRules.exceptionNotes: Hanasareru is just regular passive. You must say Hanasaserareru (Forced to speak).

cultureContext.contextText: The causative-passive is famously the 'complaining' form in Japanese corporate culture. It is heavily used in izakayas (pubs) when employees complain about what their boss 'forced' them to do (Nomikai ni ikasaremashita - I was forced to go to the drinking party).
dosList: Use causative-passive to vent about difficult requirements or strict rules.
dontsList: Do not use causative to offer help. 'Watashi ni sasete kudasai' (Please let me do it) is fine, but 'Watashi ga sasemasu' sounds incredibly arrogant.

summary: Causative = Make/Let do (Tabesaseru / Nomaseru). Causative-Passive = Forced to do (Tabesaserareru / Nomasareru).

checkpointQuestions.questionText: What is the causative form (make do) of 'Kaku' (to write)?
What is the short causative-passive form (forced to write) of 'Kaku'?
If the boss makes you work overtime, are you 'saseru' or 'saserareru'?
checkpointQuestions.expectedAnswer: させられる (Saserareru - Forced to do)

adminCorrectAnswerSet.conceptSummary: Mastering the most complex verb conjugations for power dynamics.
importantFacts: The causative form can mean 'make' OR 'let', depending on context. But causative-passive only ever means 'forced'.
adminCorrectAnswerSet.expectedUnderstanding: Learner must memorize the phonetic patterns of these long conjugations.

feedbackRules.onPass: Incredible! You have tackled the most difficult grammar point in Japanese.
feedbackRules.onFail: Review the U-verb rules. Remember the vowel shifts from 'u' to 'a'. Iku -> Ika-seru.
feedbackRules.onPartial: Good start. Practice the short causative-passive form for U-verbs to sound much more natural.

audioAssets.audioTranscript: たべさせる。のませる。ははは こどもに やさいを たべさせました。させる。こさせる。ぶちょうは わたしに ざんぎょうを させました。たべさせられる。わたしは ははに やさいを たべさせられました。のまされる。いかされる。わたしは くすりを のまされました。

commonMistakes: Saying 'Hanasareru' to mean forced to speak. Hanasareru means 'to be spoken'. You must say 'Hanasaserareru'.
